speaker: interviewer
text: On the-- on February the 8th, 1996, interviewing Joseph Carver in London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so I started going to evening classes in the City of London College of Commerce. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went out a lot. I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I met her at a bus stop. But I was very shy to approach her. And one of my friends-- this was my most very friend-- he said, I've got to go and deliver a parcel which was given me by a friend of mine to a family. Will you come with me? I said, oh, I don't know these people. Ah, he says, come. We won't stay long there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there along with him. And as the door opened, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the man who opened the door was very nice fellow. He called me in. And he said to the other fellow, is this fellow Jewish? He said, of course he is Jewish. Oh, he says, that's all right. So I'm looking out. And the girl of my dreams came down.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my wife, whom I met at the bus stop. And she was actually washing her hair. So I introduced myself. And I said, I've seen you before. I always see you at the bus. Oh, she says, I've never seen you before. Because obviously, she was not looking for me. But I told her. And then we had a date. I think we went to a show in the Palladium. Then we arranged to meet other places. And from there, you see, it took on. And we were married within nine months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you told her about your past?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. I wanted to leave this behind me. It only came out in the last few years-- actually, in my retirement. I never discussed it. I didn't want her to think of it. I knew I had a job to do. I had three children to keep and a wife. And life had to go on. And so, it wasn't all a bed of roses. We had ups and downs. Things were difficult, very, very hard-- especially if you have got no family, and you're here in the country on your own. But thank God we made it. We got three daughters. And we got three grandchildren. And we have been very, very happy. And now in my retirement, I do a lot of  welfare work, voluntary work with mentally handicapped people, old-age pensioners like myself, and people who are not so fortunate as ourselves. We try to help them to alleviate their illness, their predicaments, as best as we can. General hobbies is writing-- not only about the past but also things that happened in history, and now and again snippets of news. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What effects do you think the Holocaust has had on shaping up your life in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very difficult-- not only because of the actual experience of the Holocaust, but having had the medicine of Hitler since the age of 5. So I blamed the Germans for destroying my childhood; my youth; my family life with my parents, with my brothers, my aunts, my uncles, and my cousins; and the whole Jewish nations whom they annihilated-- 6 million of us. People could never believe that a cultured people like the Germans could have committed such a monstrosity. Killing people, murdering them because they  were Jews. We didn't commit any crimes. Because we are the followers of Moses, we had to bear the indignities, the persecution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did Hitler do it? We were the scapegoat. He had nothing. He took away our property. He plundered our homes. He stripped us of everything, including our dignity. And he murdered us. So the miracle of Germany after the war was not only the Marshall Plan, what the American pumped in. It was the loot, the plunder of Jewish property that put Germany on its feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the reparations in the world, until the end of time, will never make up for the crime which they have committed against the Jewish nation. Those old Germans who have already died, and those who are still dying, they will never find peace, never. Nobody who has being actively involved in killing and murder can die a peaceful death. They are cursed. They will be cursed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, as I have made this testimony for future generation to come, to study, to find out what actually happened to me as I remember it, they will say, how comes? It sounds like a novel. It sounds like a fairy tale. But that is the truth. That is really what happened to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only to me alone. It happened to others, to some of my friends. But every house had a different story to tell. Every family has a different story. Some people were taken to the same camp. Some people were taken to a different camp. I was taken to a different place of work. Other people were taken to a different place of work. Even in the ghetto when I worked, I haven't seen anybody in the same place I worked alive. And neither have they seen me. Sometimes, when I come across a person who says, oh, I worked in the same factory. I worked downstairs. And I say, I also worked downstairs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He doesn't know me. And I don't know him. But he remembers the foreman. And I remember the name of the foreman. He remembers the director. I remember the director. So he, again, although we didn't know each other at the time, thank God we survived. We are alive. We know each other today. That's life, full of surprises.

speaker: interviewer
text: Joe, thank you very much for--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --telling me your story today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry I can't use my right arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an accident. I broke my wrist in two sections. I slipped on  the ice. And I will have to have the plaster for another six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: I hope it heals quickly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. I hope posterity will remember us and the Holocaust will be remembered so that people will never commit such atrocities in our lifetime and in times to come. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: God, I don't [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Joe, would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is my wife, Laura. This is my daughter Shirlee, my daughter Michelle, my daughter Geraldine, and my granddaughter Laura. My two grandsons are missing because they're playing tennis tonight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name is Alexander Robert and Joel Adam.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's their mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Laura's mother is?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm [? here ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Laura, is there something that you would like to say to Joe? LAURA [SCHNITZER]: Yes. I would like to say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] considering all that he's been through, I think he's been marvelous, you know. A very good father. Always protected and looked after his children and his grandchildren. And he's been a very good son-in-law as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And basically, all his friends think the world of him. And he's had his ups and downs. But with all that, he's always been cheerful [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and worked very hard. And I hope that now he's retired, he will have a bit of easy life, and a happy life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say, he's a wonderful dad, the best dad in the world. He's a wonderful human being-- very kind, caring, considerate, and very affectionate; very, very close to all the family. You know, always phoning. If he doesn't hear from us for a day or two, he's phoning in the evening, you know, what's happening? Are you OK? And he's a very caring individual. And we're very proud of him. And we love him to bits.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd just like to second that. Sorry that the boys aren't here. But you know they love--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you to bits. And they would like to have been here for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Laura, would you like to say a word?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're all-- we're all in the fan club. I'd just like to say that we didn't mention the Holocaust at all when we were children. We knew about it. And we knew our father was in concentration camps. But we were told that we mustn't say anything, because it would upset Daddy. And if there was something on television about it, we'd all sit there and all be so embarrassed for him. We'd all feel really bad. We didn't know whether to get up and walk out--

speaker: interviewee
text: But secretly, we'd buy books about it and read up on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we would. We would. But we knew that we didn't ask, because it would dredge up the past. And we didn't want to upset him. But when he joined the survivors' group a number of years ago, it was-- it was like he was a different man. You could talk about it. And everything was more out in the open. And bit by bit, we could ask questions, which was wonderful. Because it's something that we as children, and, you know, as-- my daughter, his grandchild-- got to really know about. And it makes us understand him that more, you know, certain things that happened or things that he does, we know why. And I'm just so glad that he joined that group. Because it's really been absolutely wonderful for him and for the family. It's, you know, the best thing that happened, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it easy for you to talk now, Joe, to your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud to have such a good testimony given to me! [LAUGHTER] It makes me very happy. No, I have a very, very loving wife, and loving children and grandchildren. And I'm very proud and happy about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you just get what you deserve.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you want us to know now what happened, don't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want it to be passed on. You want even Laura  to know about it, even though she's 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's easier for me now to relate than it was years ago. It's easier for me now to relate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've been typing the  articles. Because  he writes stories for the survivors' magazine. So he used to give them to me to type, because I'm a secretary. And I found it very harrowing, you know, the first time view to read all that he'd been through. But it's a huge insight into what he has been through. So, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it mustn't be forgotten, basically, must it? So what you're doing now is wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: I look forward to seeing the video.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for coming. Because you've come quite a distance through London, through the traffic jams.

speaker: interviewee
text: Through the snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through the snow, to be with--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was worth coming for.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Joe today.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we're proud to be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [INTERPOSING VOICES] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, Dad. Give Daddy, Granddad a kiss.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, darling. [KISSING] Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So now we photograph the-- the pictures and the two documents. The person on the left-hand side--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hang on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is my mother. The lady in the middle is my grandmother. And the person on the right-hand side is my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me their full names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is Sara--

speaker: interviewer
text: And her full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara Schnitzer. My grandmother was Rachel Harband. And my uncle was Henry Harband.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photograph was taken around about 1910. And down below is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Joe, could I just ask you where that top photo--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leipzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Leipzig, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Leipzig. Down below is the town hall of Leipzig-- the new town hall. And it has got, written in German, 'Regards from Leipzig.'

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the new town hall, new-- when was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- there was a old town hall as well, dating back to the 18th century.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a new one, beginning in 19th--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --fairly new one. This is the wedding photo of my late parents, my father Aron Schnitzer and my mother Sara Schnitzer. They married in March, 1920 in Leipzig. This is my late aunt, Goldina Grinberg. She lived in Manchester in 1938 till she died around about 1949-- '49, '51. She got married in London before the First World War, had three children. And she was divorced and returned back to Leipzig to her parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your aunt related to you? On your mother's side or--

speaker: interviewee
text: On my mother's side, my mother's sister. This is my cousin, Friedel Grinberg-- my aunt's daughter. That picture was taken in Leipzig in a park not far where we lived. And I think it must've been around about 1935. About 1935. That is my cousin's daughter, Lottie, Lottie Shiva. She was born in Leipzig. That was the date she joined her school in Leipzig. Unfortunately, she was killed in the Holocaust in Poland. The last address where they used to live before the war broke out was Katowice. Because as Polish Jews, they also were deported from Leipzig to Poland on the 28th of October, 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was she related to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the daughter of my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your mother's side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On my mother's side. This was my cousin, Friedel Grinberg, with her fiance, Alexander Solomon, who was a Dutch Jew. They got married by special license in Leipzig in 1937. And as a Dutch citizen, he was allowed freedom. And he took her to Holland. Both got killed in the Holocaust. They were taken to the Dutch concentration camp of Westerbork. And they have never returned back, unfortunately. They were two splendid people, marvelous people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this picture taken, Joe?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Leipzig around about 1937. 1937, yes. That's when she was courting, yes. They were engaged at that time. These two girls were our favorite cousins-- Friedel Grinberg and Annelisa Silberman. When I think of them, I think they were two sisters who got killed, never came back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They were so young, so beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are they in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in their early 20s. Maybe-- there was only one year between them. I think one was 21, one was 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when would this photograph have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure really when it was taken. It was taken in Leipzig, not far where we lived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should think it must have been taken round about-- hm. My parents got me in 1920-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes. It must have been around about [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] '35, '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's this handsome man?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me when I first came to England. Of course, after I've been convalescing here three months. So this was taken about four months later, after my arrival. I can see this pushed me up here and they put me a nice English cap. And I look like a little gentlemen on it. Yes, that's me, all right. Happy days. That's my wife and myself on our wedding day, 12th of August, 1952. We were married at the Spanish and Portuguese Jewish Synagogue in London, Bevis Marks, one of the oldest cathedral synagogues in the United Kingdom. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: That's our first baby. That's my wife with our first child, Geraldine.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was here-- what? How many months? LAURA [SCHNITZER]: About five months old. About five months old. [? By ?] five months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it taken? Which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're good.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right-hand side is my daughter Geraldine. The middle one is Shirlee. And end one on the left-hand side is Michelle. That's our little trio.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five, six, and seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were they in the picture? LAURA [SCHNITZER]: I think they were about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five, six, and seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were about-- LAURA [SCHNITZER]: Four, five, and six. Four, five, and six. Yes, four, five, and six.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it would have been taken in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- well, five, 38-- about '58, 1958.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: '58, '59? Yes, about '58, '59. There's something which we forgot to do is always write at the back the year and the month it was taken. That's something which we have to rectify. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: This is marriage to [INAUDIBLE]. That's the wedding photo of my daughter Michelle. To the left-hand side of me is my son-in-law Henry. And of course, my daughter Michelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Henry's surname? LAURA [SCHNITZER]: Golden. And this was taken in--

speaker: interviewee
text: --70-- LAURA [SCHNITZER]: 6. 6. 1976. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were also married at the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in Holland Park.

speaker: interviewer
text: And married name is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Married name is Golden, Henry and Michelle Golden. This is the wedding photo of my daughter Geraldine and my son-in-law Frank Jackson. They, too, were married at the Spanish and Portuguese synagogue in the Holland Park in 1978. This is the wedding photo of my daughter Shirlee. On the right-hand side is my son-in-law Henry Golden. Then, there's myself, my daughter Geraldine--

speaker: interviewer
text: LAURA [SCHNITZER]: The baby. Who are you holding, Joe? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's Michelle, oh, my daughter Michelle--

speaker: interviewee
text: Alex was the page boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: --With little boy, Joel. The page boy is Alexander. The bride is my daughter Shirlee. Next to her husband is Stewart. Then, my daughter Geraldine. Next to her is her husband Frank. And the little one is Laura when she was a little baby. And the one in the front is the bridesmaid, Eloise. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was taken in--

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are you holding? LAURA [SCHNITZER]: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Joel. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: You were holding your grandson, Joel.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm holding my grandson-- LAURA [SCHNITZER]: Joel. --Joel. These are my three daughters. On the right-hand side is Michelle. The one in the middle is the bride, Shirlee. And the one on the left is Geraldine. Also taken in 1987.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this, Joe?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right-hand side is my mother-in-law. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: Sally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name is? LAURA [SCHNITZER]: Sally.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sally Jacob. The bride in the middle is my daughter Shirlee. And next to her is my wife, Laura Carver. The wedding was [? solemn ?] [? nice ?] at the Beehive Lane United Synagogue in 1987.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that? Which country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Ilford.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ilford.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilford, Essex. This is the bar mitzvah photograph of my grandson, Alexander Robert Golden, which was celebrated on the 1st July, 1995. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: At the-- At the Edgware District Synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where's that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Edgware, Middlesex.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middlesex. LAURA [SCHNITZER]: [INAUDIBLE] Who's in the photo, Joe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photo is-- LAURA [SCHNITZER]: School. --my two grandsons. The one on the right is Alexander Robert. The one on the left is Joel. This was taken during school time in 1993.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: Surname is Golden. And this is a family photograph of the bar mitzvah party luncheon-- LAURA [SCHNITZER]: And it's got the date. --On Saturday, July the 1st, 1995. On the left-hand side is Mr. Carver. Next to him is Mrs. Carver-- my wife. The bar mitzvah boy--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right-hand side.

speaker: interviewee
text: --is next to it. That's Alexander. Next to him is his mummy, my daughter Michelle. Next to her is her husband, Henry. Next to Henry is Joel Adam-- our second grandson. And the last one is my-- LAURA [SCHNITZER]: [HEBREW] --[HEBREW] are what they're called-- [LAUGHS] the mother of Henry-- Mrs. Enid Golden. This was also taken on the day of the bar mitzvah party, July the 1st, 1995. From the left is my daughter Michelle-- ah, my daughter Geraldine. And the one in the middle is my granddaughter Laura. And my son-in-law on the right-hand side is Frank Jackson.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Joe.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my older brother, Mark Schnitzer, who especially came over from the States to be at the bar mitzvah party. Also July the 1st, 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in the Holocaust Gardens of Remembrance in Miami in December, 1995. On the left-hand side is myself. On the right-hand side is my brother, at a time when we decided to pray for the dead, the Kaddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are you pointing to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We pointing at the tablet where my parents and my brother's name are inscribed in memory of them. This is my official birth certificate, sent through the diplomatic channel by the German Democratic Republic, as it was then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this shows your correct age.

speaker: interviewee
text: This shows my correct age, yes. This is the Certificate of Deportation, giving the date, October 28, 1938, issued by the Jewish community in Leipzig in 1958 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as evidence of our deportation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your deportation to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland.
